JPM.reasoning: 1. MACRO REGIME: Cautious-to-transitional with specific headwinds for financials. Fed held at 3.5%-3.75% (third consecutive hold, April 29) with no cuts priced through 2026 — higher-for-longer is now the structural backdrop. US-Iran military blockade is the dominant geopolitical risk: oil prices surging, IMF projecting 4.4% headline inflation and 3.1% global growth under conflict baseline. Wiki confirms oil surge ‘added pressure on financials’ directly. This is not a clean risk-on regime for bank stocks. S&P gained ~9% in April (best month since 2020) but Dow logged 5 straight losing sessions — mixed breadth signals a transitional regime, not a confirmed rally. 2. RELATIVE STRENGTH ASSESSMENT: JPM has been an outperformer over the 21-day window (+9.54% ROC vs. a mixed market), but this is a late-cycle reading, not an early sector-rotation entry. Short-term momentum has stalled: ROC 5d = -1.2%, with price slipping below the 5d and 10d EMAs. The stock is +3.64% above its 50 SMA and holding above all long-term MAs — the uptrend structure is intact, but the short-term is in a corrective phase after the extended run. OBV diverging from price on the daily (OBV trending up while price softens) is the key accumulation signal — institutions appear to be buying the dip quietly. 3. ASYMMETRIC R/R MATH: Entry at current hourly price ~313.58. Tight stop at hourly support 311.94 (tested 38x, volume-confirmed lower at 307.29). Target: nearest major resistance cluster 320.23-320.24 (volume-confirmed resistance). Upside: 320.24 - 313.58 = $6.66 or +2.1%. Downside to tight stop: 313.58 - 311.94 = $1.64 or -0.5%. R/R = 4.1:1 using tight hourly stop — well above the 2:1 minimum bar. If stop is widened to daily support at 304.26, R/R degrades to 0.7:1 — unacceptable; tight stop discipline is mandatory for this trade. The 4.1:1 setup is attractive, but only if the hourly 311.94 level holds. 4. CAPITAL PRESERVATION FLAGS: JPM is well-capitalized (no excessive leverage flag). However, NII guidance was trimmed from $104.5B to ~$103B for full-year 2026, and Jamie Dimon himself flagged geopolitics and fiscal deficits as systemic risks. The Iran conflict scenario (Strait of Hormuz closure, oil above $100) is a direct credit-quality and consumer-demand headwind for large-cap banks. The RSI 7-day at 23.27 is deeply oversold — a mean-reversion bounce is technically justified — and the hourly shows bullish RSI divergence with a 4.2x relative volume spike suggesting institutional accumulation. The daily ADX at 43.42 with +DI (23.43) > -DI (16.85) confirms the primary uptrend is intact. MACD histogram is modestly negative (-0.29) but not deeply bearish. CONCLUSION: The short-term technical setup (RSI oversold, hourly bullish divergence, institutional volume, 4:1 R/R with tight stop) justifies a cautious bullish call. The macro regime is a headwind, not a tailwind, keeping confidence capped at 52. This is a mean-reversion bounce trade within the primary uptrend, not a new leg higher thesis. The Iran escalation remains the primary stop-thesis trigger — if oil spikes further and financials sell off sector-wide, exit immediately regardless of the chart.
